message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 2/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"Highly trained blind listeners can comprehend speech at rates up to ~22 syllables/second, roughly 2.5x to 3x the maximum rate normal-sighted listeners can follow (~8 syl/s). This validates that extreme speed-listening (the target of an aggressively speed-optimized track) is a learnable, real perceptual capability concentrated in a trained power-listener population."

**Source:** https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "blind people...may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second...exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)"

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about your query:

Blind participants can comprehend accelerated speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second, whereas the limit for sighted participants is approximately eight syllables per second.

During ultra-fast speech perception (16 syllables per second) in trained blind people, significant activation occurs in right-hemisphere primary visual cortex (V1), left-hemisphere fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar. This reveals a remarkable neural adaptation where the part of the cerebral cortex that normally responds to vision was responding to speech in blind individuals.

The research indicates that blind adults can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for "reading" texts at ultra-fast speaking rates with 16 syllables (or more) produced per second. Some studies even report higher rates, with blind people able to comprehend speech sped up to 25 syllables per second.

# Speech Comprehension Rates: Blind vs. Sighted Individuals

## Exact Statements with Numbers

**For blind individuals:**
"repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second"

**For sighted listeners:**
"an accomplishment exceeding by far the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)"

## Source Citations

These specific figures (22 syl/s for blind, 8 syl/s for sighted) are attributed to reference [17] in the article:

**Citation:** Moos, A., & Trouvain, J. (2007). "Comprehension of ultra-fast speech – blind vs. 'normally hearing' persons." *Proceedings of the 16th International Congress of Phonetic Sciences*, Volume 1, pp. 677–680.

## Article Information

- **Publication Year:** 2013
- **Journal:** *BMC Neuroscience*
- **Authors:** Susanne Dietrich, Ingo Hertrich, and Hermann Ackermann
- **Published:** July 23, 2013
- **DOI:** 10.1186/1471-2202-14-74

content.text: The primary source is confirmed. The quote is accurately reproduced, with the figures attributed to Moos & Trouvain (2007). Let me verify the Scientific American secondary source and check whether the underlying Moos & Trouvain figure or the "trained/learnable" framing is disputed or qualified anywhere.

PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"Intelligibility and acceptability of time-compressed utterances: an experimental study with blind and sighted listeners | Journal of Speech Sciences","url":"https://econtents.sbu.unicamp.br/inpec/index.php/joss/article/view/14951"},{"title":"[PDF] COMPREHENSION OF ULTRA-FAST SPEECH - BLIND VS. \"NORMALLY HEARING\" PERSONS | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/COMPREHENSION-OF-ULTRA-FAST-SPEECH-BLIND-VS.-Moos-Trouvain/d403dd5301eaff3c66d6ed3f015de47aee66e02f"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the Moos & Trouvain 2007 study on ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind individuals. Here's what I found:

## The Study

Moos and Trouvain published "Comprehension of ultra-fast speech – blind vs. 'normally hearing' persons" in the Proceedings of the 16th International Congress of Phonetic Sciences Volume 1, published by the University of Saarbrücken in 2007.

## Key Findings

The study explored how much speech can be temporally compressed and still understood by blind people who have daily practice with speech synthesis vs. sighted persons without such training.

## The 22 Syllables Per Second Limit and Individual Variation Critique

Individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second, which matches the specific metric in your query.

Regarding the critique about confounding factors and individual variation: Moos and Trouvain (2007) used as participants a group of blind performers and normal sighted non-performers, and thus the factors blindness and performance were confounded. Thus, the question whether vision might be a limitation for ultra-fast speech comprehension was not yet answered.

Later research also noted individual variation: Residual vision in terms of visual acuity in four subjects with a significant increase of post-training behavioral performance ranged from none to 100%, showing considerable individual differences in vision levels among high performers.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Speech Comprehension Rates: Blind vs. Sighted People

**Sighted people** can comprehend speech up to approximately 10 syllables per second, described as "the absolute limit of comprehension for sighted people."

**Blind people** can understand speech accelerated to 25 syllables per second—more than double the sighted threshold.

## Important Caveats

The article does **not** describe this as a trained or learned ability. Rather, it results from neurological rewiring: "blind people's brains rewire themselves" to repurpose visual cortex regions for auditory processing.

The study subjects had lost sight between ages 2-15, suggesting the ability depends on when vision loss occurs, not on training with screen readers. The researchers note that "younger people who lose sight after these connections formed" can rewire their brains most effectively.

The article mentions blind individuals using text-to-speech software at accelerated rates for practical applications, but frames this as leveraging an inherent neurological capability rather than a learned skill.

content.text:
I now have enough to assess this skeptically. Let me weigh the evidence.

**On the claim's accuracy vs. the quote:**
- The quote is reproduced accurately from the primary source (BMC Neuroscience 2013, Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann). The 22 syl/s (blind) and ~8 syl/s (sighted) figures are present and correctly stated.
- The figures themselves originate from Moos & Trouvain (2007), a phonetics conference proceedings paper — the 2013 article cites them as background, not as its own finding.

**Where the claim slightly overreaches / has caveats:**
1. The "2.5x to 3x" multiplier: 22/8 = 2.75x, which is within range. Defensible.
2. The "learnable, trained power-listener population" framing: This is the most contestable part. The primary literature attributes the ability partly to neural reorganization (visual cortex recruitment), and Scientific American frames it as neurological rewiring tied to age of vision loss, NOT primarily training. However, the Frontiers (2013) study title is literally "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization" and the Moos & Trouvain participants were blind people with "daily practice with speech synthesis" — so training/practice IS a documented component. The claim's "learnable" framing is supported, though it co-exists with a neural-plasticity component.
3. A genuine methodological caveat exists: Moos & Trouvain confounded blindness with performer status. But the broader literature (including the cited fMRI work showing trained sighted improvement is limited, and blind training studies) supports that the capability is real and concentrated in trained blind listeners.

The claim does not assert the ability is *purely* training — it says "a learnable, real perceptual capability concentrated in a trained power-listener population," which is well-aligned with the literature. The core quantitative claims are accurately sourced from a peer-reviewed primary source and independently corroborated.

This claim survives skeptical scrutiny.

input.evidence: The quote is accurately reproduced from the cited peer-reviewed primary source: Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann (2013), "Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects...", BMC Neuroscience 14:74 (verified via PMC3847124). The article states blind people "may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second... exceeding by far the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)." These figures trace to Moos & Trouvain (2007), 16th ICPhS Proceedings, whose blind participants had "daily practice with speech synthesis" — directly supporting the "learnable/trained power-listener" framing. The 22/8 ratio = 2.75x, consistent with the claimed 2.5-3x. Independently corroborated: Scientific American cites blind comprehension up to ~25 syl/s vs ~10 for sighted; a Frontiers (2013) study is titled "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization." CAVEATS (qualify but do not refute): (a) the original Moos & Trouvain figure comes from conference proceedings, not a journal, though it is repeatedly cited in subsequent peer-reviewed journal articles; (b) Moos & Trouvain confounded blindness with performer/trained status, and follow-up work attributes part of the ability to neural reorganization (visual-cortex recruitment) and age of vision loss, not training alone — so "concentrated in a trained power-listener population" is accurate but the capability also has a plasticity component. None of these contradict the core quantitative claim or its use to validate that extreme speed-listening is a real, learnable capability.
input.counterSource: https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/why-can-some-blind-people-process/ (corroborates rather than refutes: ~25 syl/s blind vs ~10 sighted, but attributes it partly to neurological rewiring/age-of-onset rather than training, mildly qualifying the "learnable" emphasis)

content.text: Claim verified as not refuted. The 22 syl/s (blind) vs ~8 syl/s (sighted) figures are accurately quoted from the peer-reviewed BMC Neuroscience (2013) primary source, trace back to Moos & Trouvain (2007), and are independently corroborated by Scientific American and Frontiers (2013). The only qualifications — the underlying figure originating in conference proceedings, the blindness/performer confound, and a neural-plasticity (not purely training) component — refine but do not refute the claim.
